title: What does India actually make, and why does it matter?
tension: India did something no large economy had done before: it moved off the farm and straight into services, skipping the factory stage that turned every earlier success story rich. That sounds like a shortcut, but it may be a trap. Manufacturing was historically the rung that lifted millions of low- and medium-skilled workers off the land into steady wage jobs. India built a world-class services sector instead, which lifts the educated few but leaves the many. And it is happening exactly when India has the youngest, largest crop of job-seekers in its history. The shape of what India makes is really a question about whether its young people will find good work.
teachingGoal: Show that an economy has a shape, not just a size, that India's shape changed in an unusual way (off the farm, into services, skipping the factory), and why that is not a neutral curiosity but the central bind of India's development: where will good jobs for a vast young workforce come from if the factory rung is missing? The reader should read the three sector shares, see the stacked-bar composition, understand the historical pattern India broke, and grasp the jobs and demographic stakes, all without over-claiming the jobs numbers that are not in this evidence.
sectionArc: What does India actually make? (the three sectors, the present-day composition, shown as a stacked bar)
How did the mix change since 1950? (agriculture's collapse as a share, the rise of services, manufacturing standing almost still)
Why did India skip the factory stage? (the classic farm-to-factory-to-services path others took; how India leapt to services after the 1990s; premature deindustrialisation)

Why is a thin factory floor a problem, not just a quirk? (manufacturing as the historic engine of mass mid-skill wage jobs; low-productivity services as the fallback; the output-vs-jobs gap, flagged honestly since the jobs share is not in this evidence)
Why does the timing make it urgent? (the demographic dividend: a young, growing working-age population needs productive jobs in exactly the window when the factory rung is missing)
Is India trying to change course? (Make in India and the PLI push to raise manufacturing's share; the outcome is still uncertain)

Is it actually starting to work? (the clearest test case: electronics and mobile-phone assembly after 2020, riding the global 'China-plus-one' shift; the hope, and the catch that assembly adds less value than full manufacturing)
So what does the shape of India's economy actually decide? (a hard landing: it is the floor plan for the kind of country, and the kind of working life, India ends up with)
mustTeach: output divides into three broad sectors: agriculture, industry (including manufacturing), services
India's composition shifted hard: agriculture's share of output fell while services' share rose
manufacturing's share of output barely moved in 75 years, which is the unusual part
the classic development path runs farm to factory to services; India largely skipped the factory stage
manufacturing historically absorbs large numbers of low-to-mid-skill workers into steady wage jobs in a way services often do not
a sector can shrink as a share of output while still employing a large share of people (flag the output-vs-jobs gap; do not state the jobs number, which is not in this evidence)

contextCards.fact: Economists have long observed a typical path of development: as a country grows richer, workers and output move first from agriculture into manufacturing, and only later into services. Britain, Germany, Japan, South Korea and China all industrialised through a manufacturing-led stage that absorbed millions of farm workers into factory jobs.
contextCards.source: Standard structural-transformation literature (Fisher-Clark three-sector model; widely established)

contextCards.fact: India is an unusual case in that it moved heavily into services, especially IT and business services after the 1990s, without first building a large manufacturing base. Economists describe this as services-led growth or 'premature deindustrialisation', and it is the backdrop to industrial-policy drives such as Make in India, launched in 2014, and the later production-linked incentive (PLI) schemes.
contextCards.source: Standard Indian development-economics literature; 'premature deindustrialisation' (widely discussed); Make in India (Govt of India, 2014); PLI schemes

contextCards.fact: Labour-intensive manufacturing has historically been the rung that moves large numbers of low- and medium-skilled workers from low-productivity farm work into steady, higher-productivity wage employment. High-end services such as IT absorb relatively few, mostly educated, workers, while the services most ex-farm workers enter (petty retail, transport, security, construction-linked work) are often low-productivity and informal. This is why economists worry about a growth path that is strong in output but thin in manufacturing.
contextCards.source: Standard development-economics literature on manufacturing and employment (widely established)

contextCards.fact: India has one of the world's largest and youngest working-age populations, and economists speak of a 'demographic dividend': a one-time window, lasting a few decades, when a falling dependency ratio can lift growth, but only if the economy creates enough productive jobs for the young people entering the workforce. A growth path led by capital-light, skill-heavy services rather than labour-absorbing manufacturing makes that job creation harder.
contextCards.source: Standard demographic-dividend literature (UN / development economics); widely established

contextCards.fact: After 2020, India's production-linked incentive (PLI) schemes coincided with a global 'China-plus-one' shift, as companies tried to diversify supply chains away from China. Electronics and especially mobile-phone assembly grew fast: global brands and their contract manufacturers expanded Indian production, and mobile phones moved from being largely imported to being substantially assembled in India and even exported. It is the clearest test of whether India can finally lift its manufacturing share. The catch is that assembly (snapping together imported components) adds less domestic value than making those components, so a rising assembly count need not yet mean a deep manufacturing base.
contextCards.source: Widely reported post-2020 electronics/mobile manufacturing expansion under PLI and China-plus-one (uncontested directionally)
